entry.title: Re: SAP Analytics Cloud Connection To Sharepoint via Open Connectors
entry.content:
Hi Jiri - from what I understand the fix is still in flight with SAP in Openconnectors but there is a workaround.

1. In SAC Create a new connection of type SharePoint - give it a name different than any instance's in Openconnectors
2. Set the site address to xxxxx.sharepoint.com (replace with your company root URL)
3. Use the same app ID and secret as is used in Openconnectors - this is the one that uses the https://bocauth.us1.sapbusinessobjects.cloud:443 redirect
Set MS Oauth to AllSites.Read and the Scope = true
Save the SAC connection
Open Open connectors and F5 the instance screen.
Observe that the connection you just made in SAC is seen as an instance in Openconnectors.
Adjust the site address of the Openconnectors instance that was just created to be the full path of xxxxx.sharepoint.com/sites/xxxxxxxxxx - the full path of the site you want to hit.
Put in the secret for your app - the one using the https://bocauth.us1.sapbusinessobjects.cloud:443 redirect (the one you used in your SAC connection)
Press Update - DO NOT RE-AUTHENTICATE
Open the connection again in SAC and observe that the site address in the connection has changed to be xxxxx.sharepoint.com/sites/xxxxxxxxxx - which should be the full path you just put in in Openconnectors.

Test your SAC connection

Hope this helps

entry.title: Design Question – Compensation vs Variable Pay
entry.content:
HI Expert,

I have a design question regarding an AIP requirement and would appreciate your thoughts.

The client has indicated that AIP is generally based on the employee's final salary and is prorated based on hire date. However, there is one exception: if an employee moves from a Non-Executive employment type to an Executive employment type during the performance year, the AIP is prorated based on the time spent in each employment type. The target bonus and the use of individual performance may also differ between Non-Executive and Executive employees.

Salary changes, FTE changes, and other events do not generate proration; only hire date and the Non-Executive to Executive employment type change.

Based on this requirement, would you still consider Compensation as the best fit, or would you lean toward Variable Pay due to the historical employment type proration? Also, from a Variable Pay perspective, could we use a single salary basis (final salary) and only prorate by employment type history, or would salary history also need to be considered?

entry.title: Re: Product Adhoc Movement from Resource EWM
entry.content:
I encountered the same problem. Perhaps it's too late for you, but for anyone who could experience the same situation, the key is to move the product to a bin that admits materials without HU, like a GI-ZONE (Goods Issue Zone). A bin-to-bin transfer using the Warehouse Process Type S999 allows you to move the stock out of the resource, and then you can pack the product (/SCWM/PACK) once you've confirmed the WT. 

To create the WT, you've to go to Warehouse Monitor -> Stock and Bin -> Resources, select the Resource that is keeping your precious product and then select Available Stock. You'll be able to create the WT there.

 

Sorry the captures are in Spanish, but you get the key elements!

Hope it can help 🙂

entry.title: Calling Claude from ABAP: How I Wired SAP AI Core into a Real Business Scenario
entry.content:
The Itch I Had to Scratch

I was working on a module where users needed to search through hundreds of business roles using a classic SAP filter panel — dropdowns, input fields, coded values. Fine. But I kept thinking: what if someone could just type what they want in plain English?
 
"Show me high-sensitivity finance roles in production."
 
That sentence maps to at least four filter fields, each with a coded value the user has no reason to know. The UI couldn't do it. The roadmap wasn't going to do it soon either. So I decided to find out whether I could wire SAP AI Core — specifically Claude — directly into ABAP, in an afternoon, as a proof of concept. The answer was yes. Here's how it works, and more importantly, why the pattern is useful far beyond my specific use case. SAP AI Core exposes a REST API. ABAP can make HTTP calls via CL_HTTP_CLIENT using SM59 RFC destinations. AI Core uses OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials for authentication.
 
So, the flow is:
 
ABAP → OAuth token endpoint (SM59 destination) → get bearer token → ABAP → AI Core inference endpoint (second SM59 destination) → send prompt → parse JSON response back into ABAP structures.
 
That's it. No middleware, no BTP sidecar, no external service. Pure ABAP.
 
Step 1: Two SM59 Destinations
 
Everything starts in SM59 (Transaction for RFC/HTTP destination configuration). You need two HTTP destinations:
 
AI_CORE_TOKEN — OAuth 2.0 token endpoint
AI_CORE_API — Model inference endpoint
 
The token destination needs no authentication (you'll pass credentials in the body). The API destination uses the bearer token you fetch at runtime. Both use HTTPS — make sure your SAP system has the correct SSL certificates imported via STRUST.
 
Note: The deployment ID in the API path is specific to your AI Core deployment. In my case I was using Claude via Bedrock through AI Core's proxy.
 
Step 2: Fetching the OAuth Token in ABAP
 
The OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials flow is straightforward: POST your client_id and client_secret as form-encoded body, get back a JSON response containing access_token.
 
METHOD get_bearer_token.
  DATA lo_http TYPE REF TO if_http_client.

  cl_http_client=>create_by_destination(
    EXPORTING destination = 'AI_CORE_TOKEN'
    IMPORTING client      = lo_http
    EXCEPTIONS OTHERS     = 6 ).

  IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  lo_http->request->set_method(
    if_http_request=>co_request_method_post ).
  lo_http->request->set_header_field(
    name  = 'Content-Type'
    value = 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' ).

  " Client credentials flow
  DATA(lv_body) =
    |grant_type=client_credentials| &
    |&client_id={ c_client_id }| &
    |&client_secret={ c_client_secret }| &
    |&resource_tenant_id={ c_tenant_id }|.

  lo_http->request->set_cdata( lv_body ).
  lo_http->send( ).
  lo_http->receive( ).

  DATA(lv_response) = lo_http->response->get_cdata( ).
  lo_http->close( ).

  " Extract token from JSON
  FIND FIRST OCCURRENCE OF REGEX
    '"access_token"\s*:\s*"([^"]+)"'
    IN lv_response
    SUBMATCHES rv_token.
ENDMETHOD.
 
Step 3: Calling the Model
 
With a token in hand, calling the inference endpoint is a standard HTTP POST. The important detail for Claude is the request body format — Claude uses a system / messages split rather than a single flat prompt array:
METHOD call_ai_core.
  DATA lo_http TYPE REF TO if_http_client.

  cl_http_client=>create_by_destination(
    EXPORTING destination = 'AI_CORE_API'
    IMPORTING client      = lo_http
    EXCEPTIONS OTHERS     = 6 ).

  lo_http->request->set_method( 'POST' ).
  lo_http->request->set_header_field(
    name  = 'Authorization'
    value = |Bearer { iv_token }| ).
  lo_http->request->set_header_field(
    name  = 'Content-Type'
    value = 'application/json' ).
  lo_http->request->set_header_field(
    name  = 'AI-Resource-Group'
    value = 'default' ).

  DATA(lv_body) =
    |\{| &
    |"anthropic_version":"bedrock-2023-05-31",| &
    |"max_tokens":1024,| &
    |"system":"{ escape_for_json( build_system_prompt( ) ) }",| &
    |"messages":[| &
    |  \{"role":"user","content":"{ escape_for_json( iv_prompt ) }"\}| &
    |]| &
    |\}|.

  lo_http->request->set_cdata( lv_body ).
  lo_http->send( ).
  lo_http->receive( ).

  rv_result = lo_http->response->get_cdata( ).
  lo_http->close( ).
ENDMETHOD.
 
The AI-Resource-Group header is mandatory — AI Core uses it to route to the correct deployment context.
 
Step 4: The Prompt Engineering Part (Where the Real Work Is)
 
This is where I spent most of my time, and where I think the most interesting learning sits.
 
The goal: Given a free-text user query, return a predictable, structured JSON object that my ABAP code can deserialize directly into a typed structure.
 
The technique is called constrained structured output via prompt engineering. You tell the model exactly what schema to follow, exactly what coded values are valid, and you instruct it to return only valid JSON — no markdown, no preamble.
 
A simplified version of my system prompt:
 
You are a filter interpreter for an SAP application.
Convert natural language queries into structured JSON filter parameters.

Rules:
- Only use the exact filter fields and values listed below.
- For coded fields use the exact codes shown.
- Today is [injected at runtime via sy-datum].
- Return ONLY valid JSON. No markdown. No text outside JSON.

=== AVAILABLE FILTER FIELDS ===

Field: "role_type"
Type: CODED
Values:
  "SIN" = Single Role
  "COM" = Composite Role
  "BUS" = Business Role

=== OUTPUT FORMAT ===
{
  "filters": [
    {"field": "role_type", "operator": "EQ", "value": "SIN", "label": "Role Type = Single"}
  ],
  "interpretation": "Plain English summary of what was understood",
  "confidence": 90,
  "warnings": "Any assumptions, or empty string"
}
 
Step 5: Parsing the Response Back Into ABAP
 
The Claude response wraps the model output in a JSON envelope:
{
  "content": [
    {"type": "text", "text": "{...your structured JSON...}"}
  ]
}

 

I extract the inner JSON with a regex, unescape it, then deserialize it with /UI2/CL_JSON:
METHOD extract_json_from_response.
  FIND FIRST OCCURRENCE OF REGEX
    '"text"\s*:\s*"((?:[^"\\]|\\.)*)"'
    IN iv_raw_response
    SUBMATCHES rv_json.

  REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '\"' IN rv_json WITH '"'.
  REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '\n' IN rv_json WITH ' '.
  REPLACE ALL OCCURRENCES OF '\\' IN rv_json WITH '\'.
ENDMETHOD.

 

Then the deserialization:
/ui2/cl_json=>deserialize(
  EXPORTING json        = lv_json
            pretty_name = /ui2/cl_json=>pretty_mode-camel_case
  CHANGING  data        = ls_result ).
 
/UI2/CL_JSON handles camelCase to snake_case mapping automatically when you use pretty_mode-camel_case. No manual field mapping needed.
 
The Result
A single public method call:
DATA(ls_result) = zcl_nl_filter_poc=>parse_nl_to_filter(
  iv_query = 'Show me high sensitivity composite roles in the production release' ).

IF ls_result-success = abap_true.
  " ls_result-filter_json     -> ready to apply as filter conditions
  " ls_result-interpretation  -> show user what was understood
  " ls_result-confidence      -> 0-100, warn if below threshold
  " ls_result-warnings        -> surface assumptions to user
ENDIF.
 
Wiring It Into a Fiori App
The class works as a standalone utility, but the real payoff comes when you surface it in a real UI. I exposed it through a RAP custom action on an OData V4 service — the action receives the natural language query as a parameter, calls parse_nl_to_filter, and writes the result back to the entity.
 
On the Fiori side, the controller calls invokeAction on the binding context, passes the search field value, and OData V4 side effects take care of refreshing the UI automatically once the action returns. No manual model refresh needed. 
 
And this is how it looked like!!
 

 

Final Thought
The thing that surprised me most was how little plumbing was needed. Two SM59 destinations, one HTTP client call for auth, one for inference, a regex to unpack the response. The bulk of the work was the prompt itself — which is a different kind of engineering than I'm used to, but a genuinely interesting one.
 
If you're an ABAP developer wondering whether AI is "for you" or only for the frontend teams and architects with BTP budgets — it's for you too. The tools are already there.

entry.title: Solving SmartRecruiter's Salary Calculation Gap for Offer Letters Using SAP Integration Suite (CPI)
entry.content:
The Business Problem

In many organizations, offer letters must present a full salary breakdown — Cost to Company (CTC), Basic salary, HRA, and Special Allowance — each derived from the gross CTC using pre-defined calculation logic. SmartRecruiter, as a standard product, does not natively support this on-the-fly salary computation. Recruiting stakeholders are left to manually populate individual salary components, which introduces risk of error and inconsistency.

The requirement is straightforward: when a recruiter enters the CTC for a candidate, the system should automatically calculate the salary components and pre-populate the offer fields — without any manual intervention and without a custom build inside SmartRecruiter.

Solution Overview

The solution leverages the Webhook capability native to SmartRecruiter and the orchestration power of SAP Integration Suite. The approach is event-driven: when a recruiter updates the CTC field (or moves a candidate to the next stage), a webhook fires and triggers an integration flow in CPI that performs the salary calculation and upserts the results back to SmartRecruiter via REST APIs.

Webhook Setup

Two webhook events are subscribed to in SmartRecruiter to cover both update scenarios:

  • application.fields.updated — fires when a field value on an application is changed
  • application.status.updated — fires when the candidate is moved to a new stage

Either event reliably signals that the CTC may have been updated and that the calculation should run. The webhook payload carries the candidate ID and job requisition ID, which are used as keys in subsequent API call

Step 1 — Configuration in SmartRecruiter

Custom Application Fields

CTC -

HRA -

Similarly define other pay components required for calculations -

CTC as application field.

Rest of pay components as offer fields.

For ex - Four custom fields are configured in SmartRecruiter to hold the salary components:

  • CTC — Gross Cost to Company (input field; marked as mandatory for the recruiter to populate)
  • HRA — House Rent Allowance (output; auto-populated via CPI)
  • Basic — Basic Salary (output; auto-populated via CPI)
  • Special Allowance — (output; auto-populated via CPI)

Since CTC is the trigger field, it is set as mandatory. The recruiting stakeholder is instructed to update CTC either at the application stage or when moving the candidate to the offer stage.

Recruiting stakeholder fills the value of CTC field -

Step 2 — Retrieving the CTC Value from SmartRecruiter

How Fields Are Stored in SmartRecruiter

In SmartRecruiter, custom fields are represented as candidate properties — effectively key-value pairs attached to the candidate entity. Each field has a unique Property ID (UUID) that is referenced in API calls.

Retrieving Candidate Properties

To retrieve all property values for a candidate, including the CTC field, the following GET request is used:

How to retrieve the CTC value - 

 

Step 3 — Salary Calculation in SAP Integration Suite (CPI)

Once the webhook fires and the CPI iFlow is triggered, the integration flow performs the following sequence:

  • Extracts the candidate ID and job requisition ID from the webhook payload
  • Calls the SmartRecruiter properties API to fetch the current CTC value for the candidate
  • Applies the pre-configured salary calculation logic within the Groovy script or message mapping step in CPI
  • Derives HRA, Basic, and Special Allowance values from the CTC
  • Prepares the upsert payload with the computed values
Integration Note

The calculation logic (e.g., percentage splits for HRA, Basic, Special Allowance) is externalized in CPI and can be adjusted without touching SmartRecruiter configuration. This keeps the business rules in a governed, version-controlled integration layer.

Step 4 — Upserting Calculated Values Back to SmartRecruiter

With the calculated salary components ready, CPI writes the values back to the SmartRecruiter candidate record using a PUT/PATCH call scoped to both the candidate and the specific job requisition:

The payload specifies each Property ID and its newly calculated value. All three output fields — HRA, Basic, and Special Allowance — are upserted in a single call.

Step 5 — Verifying the Results

After the upsert, a GET call to the candidate properties endpoint confirms that the calculated values are now stored correctly:

Step 6 — Creating the Offer in SmartRecruiter

Once the candidate properties are populated, the recruiter proceeds to create the formal offer letter inside SmartRecruiter. Because the salary components have already been written to the candidate record, the offer template picks them up automatically — the recruiter sees pre-populated values for all compensation fields, including Special Allowance, without any manual calculation.

Handling Renegotiation Scenarios

Real-world recruiting frequently involves renegotiation. If a candidate requests a revised offer, the flow handles this gracefully:

  • The candidate is moved back to the Offered stage (this can be done programmatically via the SmartRecruiter API)
  • The recruiting stakeholder updates the CTC value
  • The stage change or field update automatically fires the webhook again
  • CPI recalculates all salary components and upserts the new values to SmartRecruiter
  • A new offer is created with the revised compensation breakdown
Key Benefit
The renegotiation cycle is entirely self-service for the recruiter — no integration team involvement is needed after the initial setup. Simply updating the CTC triggers the full recalculation automatically.
 

Conclusion

SmartRecruiter's gap in automated salary calculation for offer letters is fully addressable through a lightweight, event-driven integration pattern using SAP Integration Suite (CPI). The solution is non-invasive — it requires no customization of SmartRecruiter beyond standard field configuration and webhook setup — and it delivers a seamless recruiter experience.

Key takeaways from this implementation:

  • SmartRecruiter Webhooks provide a reliable event trigger for real-time integration
  • Candidate fields are accessible as properties via standard REST APIs
  • SAP Integration Suite (CPI) serves as the calculation engine, keeping business rules externalized and maintainable
  • The upsert API allows CPI to write back computed values directly to SmartRecruiter offer fields
  • The pattern supports renegotiation cycles without manual intervention

 

Thanks

Kunal

Product Success

SAP RIG

entry.title: Re: June Developer Challenge - CAP Knowledge - Task 04 - Namespaces, contexts and name prefixes
Re: Cannot Continue with Migration because Microsoft JDBC Driver for SQL Server is connected
entry.content: Close all SAP Business One client sessions connected to the company database. Stop the SAP Business One Service Layer service temporarily: Open Windows Services (services.msc) Find SAP Business One Service Layer Right-click → Stop Open SQL Server Management Studio (SSMS) and connect to the database server. Open Activity Monitor: Right-click the server instance in Object Explorer → Activity Monitor Or press Ctrl+Alt+A Expand the Processes section and look for any process where: Database = your company database name Application = Microsoft JDBC Driver for SQL Server Right-click that process → Kill Process → confirm. Verify no remaining JDBC connections exist by refreshing the process list. Go back to SAP Business One and retry the migration (Migrate Account Assignments & Inventory G/L Account Determination Settings). Once the migration completes successfully, restart the SAP Business One Service Layer service.

entry.title: SAC Modeler – postingPeriod Not Mapped to Calendar Month for Company Code 2000
entry.content:
In SAP Analytics Cloud, the Balance Sheet report for Company Code 2000 is showing missing/incomplete data. Upon investigation, it has been identified that the source column "postingPeriod" is not mapped to the "Calendar Month" dimension in the Data Foundation (Facts) within the Modeler.

This mapping exists and is working correctly for Company Codes 1000 and 3000, confirming this is isolated to Company Code 2000.

entry.title: Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Great breakdown on the implementation steps. Having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I’d add one critical nuance: the go-live week is where most SMBs lose the most money, and it’s almost never a technical issue — it’s a data readiness and real-time visibility problem.

Three failure patterns I see repeatedly:

1. Master data validation is skipped under time pressure.

Teams migrate from Tally/legacy spreadsheets and the item master is “good enough” on cutover night. Three weeks later, inventory reports are useless because unit of measure conversions weren’t standardized. The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved. This is where having real-time data visibility during the transition is critical — you need to see discrepancies as they happen, not in a post-mortem report.

2. User training is treated as a checkbox.

SMB owners assume the consultant will “handle it.” What actually happens: 3 PowerPoint slides, a rushed demo, and a team that can log in but can’t process a goods receipt. The result: workarounds that corrupt data, followed by 6 months of cleanup. Better model: identify 2–3 power users per department in week 1. Train them deeply. They become your go-live support army.

3. No cutover rollback plan.

If something breaks on go-live night, what’s the decision tree? Who decides to rollback vs. push through? This sounds obvious but almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos before the inevitable rollback — which costs more than if you’d planned for it.

The Go-Live Checklist that actually works:

  • Parallel run confirmation: Both systems running simultaneously for at least 5 business days. Cross-check every transaction — especially batch costs and inventory valuations.
  • Data freeze window: No master data changes 48 hours before cutover. Lock item masters, vendor records, and G/L mappings.
  • Rollback decision criteria: Document who decides, under what conditions, and the exact rollback procedure. Don’t improvise this at 2 AM on cutover night.
  • Go-live support team: Internal champion + consultant available for 72 hours straight. Power users in each department as first responders.
  • First-week close check: Finance runs month-end in SAP B1 within 5 days of go-live. If they can’t close the books, you have a data problem that needs immediate attention.
  • Batch cost gap analysis: For pharma and manufacturing — run a full batch cost comparison between your legacy system and SAP B1 within the first week. Any gap >1% in material costs signals a configuration issue that compounds over time.

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run before go-live where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously. Painful? Yes. Expensive? Yes. Cheaper than data recovery after a bad cutover? Far less.

Teams that skip this checklist average 3–4 weeks of post-go-live disruption. Teams that use it average 3–4 days. The difference is almost always data readiness and having real-time visibility into gaps before they become production problems.

What specific module are you struggling with? Happy to go deeper.

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs

entry.title: SAP Developer News, June 4th, 2026
entry.content:
Items

ADT for VS Code available on VS Code Marketplace
- Official announcement blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/abap-development-tools-for-visual-studio-code-is-now-available-on-the-vs/ba-p/14402120
- FAQ https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/abap-development-tools-for-visual-studio-code-is-now-available-on-the-vs/ba-p/14402120
- GitHub Tutorial https://github.com/SAP-samples/abap-platform-rap130/tree/main
- VS Code Marketplace Link https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=SAPSE.adt-vscode

SAP BTP ABAP Environment – release 2605
- Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-btp-abap-environment-release-2605/ba-p/14403929
- What’s New for SAP BTP ABAP environment https://help.sap.com/whats-new/7a822d3bcaa74f31b98fa315601e9c96?locale=en-US
- What’s New in Generative AI in ABAP Cloud https://help.sap.com/whats-new/4d359a7f8e8248e18f98e85ea0b9d83e?locale=en-US

June Developer Challenge details
- The challenge https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-cap-blog-posts/june-developer-challenge-cap-knowledge/ba-p/14405157

Did You Know? #19 Standardize Class Definition
- ABAP Cleaner https://github.com/SAP/abap-cleaner

Chapter titles

00:00 Intro
00:07 ADT for VS Code available on VS Code Marketplace
02:01 SAP BTP ABAP Environment – release 2605
03:20 June Developer Challenge details
04:54 Did You Know? #19 Standardize Class Definition

Transcript

[Intro]
This is the SAP Developer News for June the 4th, 2026.

[Sheena]
Hello, ABAP Developers. We have an exciting news this week. The first version of ABAP Development Tools for Visual Studio Code extension is available in the VS Code Marketplace. This marks a significant step in modernizing the ABAP Development experience by bringing ABAP Development to one of the most widely used IDEs in the developer community. The initial release focuses on the ABAP Cloud development model and supports key artifacts such as ABAP classes, interfaces, CDS artifacts, service definitions, service binding, and behavior definitions. This extension includes essential development features such as code completion, navigation, syntax checking, debugging, ABAP unit integration, transport management, and repository search. A key highlight is that developers building RAP applications can now work on both the backend ABAP artifacts and the frontend SAP Fiori or SAPUI5 application within the same development environment. The extension also includes a built-in model context protocol or MCP server. This enables AI agents available in Visual Studio Code such as Microsoft Copilot to interact with ABAP development objects and assist developers directly within their workflow. To get started, install VS code, add the ADT extension from the marketplace, connect it to your ABAP system and you're ready to go. SAP has also published a GitHub tutorial showing how the built-in ADT MCP server can be used with AI agents to create a simple RAP application. Don't forget to also take a look at this FAQ document, which is also included in the blog post. This is just the beginning. More capabilities and ABAP development objects are planned for upcoming releases. So stay tuned.

[Shilpa]
Hello, ABAP developers. the SAP BTP ABAP environment 2605 has been released and this blog post by Nora Klemp provides an overview of the delivered features. Here are some of the key highlights. In ABAP language, Boolean expression is now supported. Yes, this was widely discussed and requested and it has now been supported. In ABAP development tools, there is a signature comparison when you are migrating from DDIC based views to CDS view entity. In ABAP restful application programming model draft is now supported in cds table entities there are two new attributes in feature control one is read-only create as well as mandatory update in analytics the WYSIWYG editor of the review booklets now supports multiple data providers in ATC a new variant is introduced to perform clean core analysis in both SAP ECC as well as SAP S/4HANA systems in landscape portal you can use Deploy Product App to deploy or update a specific product version and specific system. You can check out all the new features in What's New for SAP BTP ABAP Environment page. The new AI features is listed separately in What's New for ABAP Generative AI for ABAP Cloud. Check it out.

[DJ]
The June Developer Challenge is underway. and this month we're focusing on exercising our grey matter in order to maintain some level of cognitive discipline in this new era that's right and in the challenge we're diving into the CAP documentation to learn what's there and how to navigate to it but perhaps more importantly to learn the names for and understand the fundamentals of concepts features language constructs and more all of which make CAP what it is why because knowing what things are called and how they relate and what they do and where to find more information is the foundation of understanding and how it's built and understanding is more important than ever today. So this month there's a new question published on every working day that's 22 days in total 22 questions Each question focuses on a particular aspect or topic or area of CAP and where the answers can be found in the CAP documentation, in Capire. And also, after each day's question is done, there are links to further reading added to the end of the question post for those of you who want to learn more. It's like a five-minute mini -workout for your brain each day. What are you waiting for? See you online.

[Sheena]
Did you know that the standardized class definition feature in ABAP Cleaner helps keep class definitions consistent, improving code readability and maintainability? Usually, in ABAP class definitions, the ordering and layout of options such as public, private sections inheriting from, etc., can vary across developers and projects, leading to inconsistent code structure and reduced readability. To address this issue, ABAP Cleaner has a rule, Standardize Class Definition. It has various options like indentation, specifying number of spaces, and splitting the statement to one or multiple lines. For example, here if we select public to be in a separate line, the class definition gets rewritten with public in next line across all class definitions in your code. Similarly, for inheriting from and create statements. Now, you can see that the various class definitions within the code has the same format, thereby improving readability and maintainability and hence following SAP best practices.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA System Conversion: Reduce the memory footprint of Finance obsolete tables
entry.content:
When converting to SAP S/4HANA, Finance teams often expect an immediate reduction in database size—but this doesn’t always happen. After the System Conversion, the system still contains obsolete ECC finance data and legacy tables, which means the overall data footprint may remain unchanged at first. To take full advantage of SAP S/4HANA’s simplified finance data model, it’s important to perform a proper post‑conversion clean up.

SAP recommends removing content from obsolete tables with notes:

SAP Note 2190137 SAP S/4HANA Finance: Reduce memory footprint of obsolete tables. SAP Note  2569435  MM-IM: Save DB memory and remove data that is not covered by Information Lifecycle Management.

Customers must not only implement the relevant SAP Notes but also complete any manual corrections or follow-up activities. These extra steps are often essential for fully realizing the desired results, including data cleanup, system optimization, and maintaining performance improvements.

Additionally, the Finance and Basis teams should identify any remaining outdated data after conversion. SAP Note 2661837 provides guidance on preventing legacy structures from occupying unnecessary database space. Once cleanup is complete, running the SAP Note 1872170 is crucial to reassess the data footprint, which supports more accurate capacity planning and cost management.

From a Finance perspective, this cleanup is a key step—not just a technical task. Reducing unnecessary data improves system performance, supports faster reporting and financial close processes, and helps control infrastructure costs. A successful S/4HANA conversion doesn’t end with go‑live; cleaning up obsolete Finance data is what unlocks the real value of the move.

Conclusion

In summary, a successful SAP S/4HANA Finance conversion involves more than just completing the technical migration and going live. While the database may not immediately shrink after the upgrade, real advantages come from cleaning up outdated Finance data and legacy tables. By following SAP’s recommended notes and collaborating with the Finance and Basis teams, organizations can eliminate unnecessary data, enhance system performance, and gain clearer insights into their actual data volume.

Please “like” if you found this blog post interesting!  

Please add in the comment section any additional topics you think are valuable to have included in this and future blog posts.

– Brought to you by the S/4HANA RIG

entry.title: PRINT AFTER TECO

Hi everyone,

I have a question about printing maintenance orders after they reach TECO status.

In our process, once an order is TECO, IW3D no longer allows us to print the shop papers. The current workaround is to remove TECO, print the order, and then set TECO again.

I was wondering if anyone has found a better way to handle this.

My goal is simply to be able to generate the same documentation for historical work orders without having to reopen them. I'm not really looking to change status management or use BS22 if possible.

Has anyone dealt with a similar situation? Did you find a standard SAP solution, a report, an enhancement, or another approach that worked well?

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Re: Datasphere - Scheduling task chains skipping holidays
entry.content: @enrique_franco I am not aware of a native option in SAP Datasphere for scheduling task chains that can automatically skip public holidays. The available scheduling options are limited to periodic schedules. However, your proposed solution is feasible so an external enterprise scheduler can utilize a holiday calendar to trigger the Datasphere task chain only on working days. Datasphere supports the execution of task chains through the Tasks REST API or the command-line interface (CLI).

entry.title: Re: SAC Modeler – postingPeriod Not Mapped to Calendar Mo...
entry.content: @Apurva_Thambichetty Since all three company codes are loaded through the same import job into the same model and Data Foundation, the mapping of the source columns should not be configured differently for Company Code 2000. This suggests that the issue is more likely related to the records loaded for Company Code 2000. For instance, some fields may be empty or null for those records. Additionally, the period format may differ from that of the working company codes, preventing values from being recognized as valid Calendar Month members. It is also possible that rows were rejected or assigned to an unassigned member during the import process.I recommend filtering the import preparation view to focus on Company Code 2000 and comparing the raw values with those of Company Codes 1000 and 3000. Furthermore, review the import job’s mapping and transform history, as well as the details panel for any data quality warnings or rejected records.

entry.title: From Clicks to Conversations: Reimagining Customer Onboarding Through Agentic AI
entry.content:
At SAP's Bay Area Developer Community (DCom) event, the CloudOps and Deployment Hub (DH) teams jointly showcased how SAP's Autonomous Enterprise vision can be realized through Agentic AI.  The demonstration brought together several key SAP strategic initiatives including Joule, SAP Business Suite, Model Context Protocol (MCP), and SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) to transform a traditionally complex onboarding process into an intelligent, conversational, and autonomous experience.

When customers purchase a SAP Business Suite solution, onboarding often requires multiple provisioning, configuration, and validation activities before they can begin realizing value from their investment.  Traditionally, customers must navigate various applications, complete forms, provide technical configuration details, and manually coordinate onboarding activities. While effective, these processes can be time-consuming and require specialized knowledge.  Our goal was to explore a different approach: What if customers could simply communicate their intent while intelligent agents handled the onboarding process?

The demonstration begins with a simple request through Joule:

"Start onboarding SKU 0899743."

The SKU represents a purchased SAP Business Suite offering or a subset of SAP Business Suite offering . However, the request itself is only the starting point.  Rather than directing customers to forms or documentation, Joule initiates a guided conversation. The request is routed through Joule Digital Assistant, which orchestrates specialized Deployment Hub agents that understand the onboarding workflow associated with the purchased solution.  The agents then engage the customer in a dynamic conversation, gathering the information required to successfully provision and configure the offering. Depending on the solution, they may ask questions related to tenant configuration, deployment preferences, networking requirements, security settings, user provisioning, or other product-specific details.

As responses are provided, the agents continuously evaluate the information, determine the next required steps, and adapt the conversation until all onboarding prerequisites have been satisfied.  The result is an experience that feels less like completing a deployment workflow and more like collaborating with an experienced onboarding specialist.

AI Execution

Many AI solutions help users perform tasks more efficiently by answering questions or providing recommendations.  This demonstration focused on something more powerful: execution.  Once the required onboarding information has been collected, Deployment Hub agents orchestrate the onboarding workflow on behalf of the customer. The agents coordinate activities, invoke business services, monitor progress, and execute the deployment process required to onboard the purchased SAP Business Suite solution.

Instead of navigating complex procedures, customers focus on the desired business outcome while the system manages the operational complexity behind the scenes.  This shift from AI-assisted workflows to AI-executed workflows represents an important step toward SAP's Autonomous Enterprise vision.

Enabling Agent Actions Through MCP

A key component of the architecture is how agents interact with enterprise systems.  Rather than directly calling backend APIs, Deployment Hub agents communicate with MCP tools hosted on a SAP MCP Server. Communication is facilitated through the SAP MCP Gateway, providing a secure and scalable mechanism for exposing enterprise capabilities to AI agents.  Both the SAP MCP Gateway and SAP MCP Server are deployed on SAP BTP.

When onboarding actions need to be performed, agents invoke MCP tools through the gateway. The MCP Server then interacts with Deployment Hub backend APIs responsible for provisioning resources, configuring services, creating users, and performing onboarding activities.  This architecture provides a reusable and governed foundation for agent-driven business processes while enabling conversational AI, agent orchestration, and backend services to evolve independently.

The demonstration was made possible through a close collaboration between the CloudOps and Deployment Hub teams. At its core, this demonstration was about much more than onboarding a customer.  It was a tangible example of SAP's Autonomous Enterprise vision in action.  A customer expresses intent through Joule. Intelligent agents determine what information is needed, engage the customer through a guided conversation, invoke enterprise capabilities through MCP, coordinate backend services, and execute the onboarding workflow.  Throughout the process, the customer remains focused on the business objective while the system manages the complexity.  By combining Joule, Agentic AI, SAP Business Suite, MCP, and BTP, the CloudOps and Deployment Hub teams demonstrated how enterprise software can evolve from screen-driven workflows to intelligent, outcome-driven experiences.

As SAP continues its journey toward the Autonomous Enterprise, solutions like this offer a glimpse into a future where customers interact through natural conversations, intelligent agents orchestrate business processes, and meaningful outcomes are achieved with greater speed, simplicity, and autonomy.

entry.title: Re: Problem installing CR for Visual Studio SP40
entry.content:
R&D did find the issue and will fix it in the next Release,

Here's the work around:

I could reproduce the issue by just changing the region format to non-English, then launched the CR4VS64 installer it will get error.

The workaround is in the Control Panel\All Control Panel Items\Region, set Format to English. 

Or if user did not want to change the region format setting,   that they need extract CRforVS64_13_0_40.exe, and directly run CRforVS64_13_0_40\packageCrystalReportsForVisualStudio64.msi. However it will get the blank EULA dialog:

We will fix this issue in next SP, this should be caused by the 32bit runtime removal in CR4VS64 package.

entry.title: SAP EWM DODO — The 4 Variants You Must Know Before You Configure (DODO in Embedded S/4HANA 2025 EWM)
entry.content:
This blog is based on hands-on configuration and troubleshooting of DODO in an S/4HANA 2025 embedded EWM sandbox. All findings are from direct sandbox work — not copied from SAP Help. Screenshots are from warehouse EPG1 on S/4HANA 2025 FPS01.

Objective

The purpose of this blog is to present a practical framework for SAP EWM Direct Outbound Delivery Order (DODO) in embedded S/4HANA 2025. DODO allows creating an Outbound Delivery Order (ODO) directly in EWM without a preceding ERP delivery — EWM is the source, and S/4HANA creates the corresponding document dynamically.

Many consultants — myself included — have spent hours debugging DODO only to discover they were configuring the wrong variant entirely. This blog presents a clear 4-variant framework to help you choose the right path before you configure, and a diagnostic guide for the most common blocker: the IS_HEADER_ORG empty payload in SMQ2.

 

Background — What Is DODO?

In the standard SAP outbound flow, the sequence is: S/4HANA creates a sales order → creates an outbound delivery → distributes it to EWM → EWM executes the delivery. DODO reverses the first step: EWM creates the ODO first, and S/4HANA creates the corresponding ERP document dynamically via qRFC.

This is particularly useful in scenarios where the warehouse initiates the outbound process — for example, directly loading a production output pallet onto a shuttle truck to another plant, without waiting for an ERP-initiated delivery.

 

Section 1: The 4 DODO Variants

After researching 5 sources (SAP Help, two SAP Community blogs, an SAP Press project config guide, and direct sandbox work), I identified four distinct DODO variants in embedded S/4HANA 2025. Each has a different document type, ERP communication path, and function module called in the background.

Choosing the wrong variant is the #1 reason DODO implementations fail. The document type (OPIG vs ODSH), the ship-to partner type (customer BP vs plant BP), and the Comm. to ERP setting on the document type all determine which ERP path is followed. These are not interchangeable.

 

Variant

Doc Type

Item Type

Ship-To Partner

FM Called in Background

Use Case

1 — Customer Pickup

OPIG

ODPG

Customer BP (external)

/ SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA

External customer collects from warehouse without prior sales order

2 — Direct STO via OPIG

OPIG

ODPG

Plant BP (T001W.KUNNR)

/ SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV + /SPE/DELIVERY_CREATE_FROM_STO

Plant-to-plant STO — SAP recommended path for embedded S/4HANA

3 — Direct STO via ODSH

ODSH

ODSH

Plant BP

/ SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV + /SPE/DELIVERY_CREATE_FROM_STO

Plant-to-plant STO via ODSH doc type — requires specific Comm. to ERP setting

4 — Goods Movement Only

ODSH

ODSH

Not required

/ SCWM/ERP_GM_SYNC_UPD_S4 + /SPE/GOODSMVT_CREATE

Scrapping, cost center issue, internal consumption — no ERP delivery created

 

Let me walk through each variant in detail.

 

Variant 1: OPIG — Customer Pickup (Standard Customer Delivery)

This is the classic DODO scenario. The warehouse creates an ODO using document type OPIG (Outbound Delivery Order for Pickup) and the ship-to party is an external customer BP.

The key setting on OPIG that distinguishes it from ODSH is: Pickup = A (Mandatory). This flag tells EWM that someone is coming to collect — and when combined with a customer BP as ship-to, routes the replication to /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA, which creates a standard LE outbound delivery in S/4HANA.

 

 

 

The system flow:

  • /SCWM/PRDO: Create ODO with OPIG/ODPG, ship-to = customer BP, product + WPT
  • EWM creates warehouse order and warehouse task for picking
  • qRFC message sent to S/4HANA: /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA with IS_HEADER_ORG (Sales Org/DC/Div/ShPt)
  • S/4HANA creates LE outbound delivery (type LF) — visible in VL03N
  • Pick, confirm WT, post GI in EWM → movement type 601 in S/4HANA

 

Variant 2: OPIG — Direct STO (SAP Recommended Path for Plant-to-Plant Transfer)

This is the SAP-recommended path for EWM-triggered plant-to-plant stock transfers in embedded S/4HANA. It uses the same OPIG document type as Variant 1, but the ship-to party is a plant BP (the customer number assigned to the receiving plant in T001W.KUNNR) — not an external customer.

When EWM detects a plant BP as ship-to on an OPIG ODO (combined with the Pickup = Mandatory flag), it routes the replication via the STO function modules instead of the customer delivery FM. This is the architectural difference  not the document type, but the partner type.

Variant 2 is the path described in SAP's own blog 'Advanced Supply Chains with SAP EWM — Direct Loading of Finished Goods.' If you are implementing a plant-to-plant DODO in embedded S/4HANA 2025, start here.

 

The system flow:

  • /SCWM/PRDO: Create ODO with OPIG/ODPG, ship-to = receiving plant BP (T001W.KUNNR), assign HU at loading
  • EWM detects plant BP → routes to STO FMs
  • qRFC: /SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV → creates STO Purchase Order (type UB) in S/4HANA
  • qRFC: /SPE/DELIVERY_CREATE_FROM_STO → creates NL outbound delivery linked to STO PO
  • NL delivery distributed back to EWM for execution
  • Post GI in EWM → movement type 641 (GI for STO with delivery) in S/4HANA
  • SPED output triggers automatic inbound delivery at receiving plant

 

Variant 3: ODSH — Direct STO via ODSH Document Type

This variant uses ODSH (ODO Direct Outbound Delivery) instead of OPIG for the STO flow. It is referenced in SAP Community blogs and requires the /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE table to have an entry with Doc.Cat = J (Stock Transfer) for the ODSH delivery type.

 

 Important finding from S/4HANA 2025 sandbox: the standard ODSH document type has 'Comm. to ERP = System Default' with only three dropdown options (System Default, Via Goods Movement Interface, No ERP Involved). None of these explicitly routes to the STO FMs. Even with the /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE table correctly configured, the system continued calling /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA instead of /SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV.

In S/4HANA 2025 sandbox testing, Variant 3 remained blocked because the standard ODSH 'Comm. to ERP' field does not expose an STO routing option. If you need plant-to-plant DODO, use Variant 2 (OPIG with plant BP) which is functionally equivalent and confirmed working by SAP's own documentation.

 

Variant 4: ODSH — Goods Movement Only (No ERP Delivery)

This variant uses ODSH with ‘Comm. to ERP = B (Via Goods Movement Interface)’. No LE delivery or STO is created in S/4HANA — stock is moved via a direct goods movement posting. This is the intended path for scrapping, cost center issues, internal consumption, and VAS component reservations.

 

 

The system flow:

  • /SCWM/PRDO: Create ODO with ODSH, Comm. to ERP = B, product + WPT (no ship-to customer needed)
  • EWM creates WO + WT, pick and confirm
  • Post GI in EWM
  • Synchronous goods movement: /SCWM/ERP_GM_SYNC_UPD_S4 + /SPE/GOODSMVT_CREATE
  • Material document created in S/4HANA — no delivery — verify in MB51/MIGO

S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 sandbox observation: When creating a custom document type (ZDSH) by copying the standard ODSH, the Comm. to ERP field was greyed out and could not be changed to “B — Via Goods Movement Interface.” The root cause was not fully investigated in this session. The config path above documents the intended SAP design.

Variant 4 vs /SCWM/ADGI — when does ODSH add value?

A natural question: if /SCWM/ADGI already handles scrapping directly in EWM, why configure Variant 4 at all? For pure scrapping with no process control requirements, /SCWM/ADGI is the simpler and recommended path. However, ODSH-based Variant 4 provides architectural value in three specific scenarios: (1) Process control and workflow — ODSH creates a formal ODO with warehouse order and task, allowing a physical confirmation step before the goods movement. /SCWM/ADGI posts directly with no WT/WO. Where supervisor approval or quality hold release is required before scrapping, ODSH is the right design. (2) HU-managed stock with lifecycle requirements — where a sealed HU must be formally deactivated as part of the goods movement, the ODSH warehouse task flow handles HU lifecycle correctly. (3) Audit trail and document traceability — the ODO-to-material-document chain provides granular traceability that /SCWM/ADGI does not, which matters in pharma, food, and medical device implementations. If none of these apply, use /SCWM/ADGI.

 

Section 2: Key Configuration Steps

Below is a consolidated list of the key configuration steps required across Variants 1 and 2 (OPIG/ODPG path). Variant 4 requires only the ODSH document type change.

2.1 EWM: Define Document Type OPIG

IMG: SCM Extended Warehouse Management > EWM > Goods Issue Process > Outbound Delivery > Define Document Types for Outbound Delivery Process

Field

Value

Note

Document Type

OPIG

Standard SAP — do not copy unless required

Pickup

A — Mandatory

KEY FIELD — distinguishes OPIG from ODSH. Triggers STO routing when plant BP is ship-to.

Comm. to ERP

System Default

Do not change for Variants 1 and 2

Process Profile

/SCWM/OUT_PRD_PICKUP

Standard pickup profile

2.2 S/4HANA: Delivery Type Determination for DODO

Table: /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE

Field

Value

Doc.Cat.

J (Stock Transfer)

EWM Del. Type

* (wildcard)

Initiator

* (wildcard)

Warehouse

Your warehouse number

Del. type

ODSH

IC DlvTy

NL (intra-company)

CC DlvTy

NLCC (cross-company)

2.3 S/4HANA: Item Category Determination

Table: /SPE/DIRITM_CATG

Field

Value

EWM Doc Type / Item Type

ODSH / ODSH

IC ReplItC

NLN (intra-company)

CC ReplItC

NLC (cross-company)

2.4 S/4HANA: STO Setup (Variant 2 specific)

IMG: MM > Purchasing > Purchase Order > Set Up Stock Transport Order

  • Document type: UB
  • Supplying plant: your sending plant
  • Delivery type: NL
  • Item category: NLN

2.5 Master Data: Receiving Plant as Customer

  • T001W: CustNoPlnt = plant customer number for receiving plant
  • T001W: SO ICB = sending plant sales org (critical for IS_HEADER_ORG derivation)
  • T001W: VSTEL = shipping point for sending plant. CRITICAL: this field is populated via SPRO → Logistics General → Handling Unit Management → Basics → Materials Management → Inventory Management → HU Requirement for Storage Locations and Default Values for Deliveries. Per SAP Note 2698417: OVXC does NOT populate T001W.VSTEL (OVXC is for shipping point determination in sales orders only). Transaction OMGN does not exist in S/4HANA. See Section 3 for full diagnostic detail — this is the most commonly missed field in all DODO implementations.
  • KNVV: Extend plant customer to sending plant sales area (VKORG/VTWEG/SPART)

 

Section 3: The Most Common Blocker — IS_HEADER_ORG Empty in SMQ2

If you have configured DODO and are getting error VP201 'Customer not maintained for sales area' in SMQ2, the first thing to check is NOT the customer master. Check the IS_HEADER_ORG parameter in the SMQ2 queue container display.

SMQ2 — Inbound Queue showing /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA with VP201 error: “Customer 316 not maintained for sales area”

 

IS_HEADER_ORG is the import parameter of /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA that contains the organizational unit data for the ERP delivery: Shipping Point (ShPt), Sales Org (SOrg), Distribution Channel (DC), Division (Div), and Warehouse Number (WhN).

When IS_HEADER_ORG is empty, the system cannot determine which sales area to use for the delivery, and VP201 fires regardless of whether the customer is correctly extended to a sales area. The customer master is not the problem — the derivation logic upstream is the problem.

Do not spend time extending customers to additional sales areas when IS_HEADER_ORG is blank. Fix the derivation first.

The Fix: How to Correctly Populate T001W.VSTEL

This is the most misunderstood step in DODO configuration. Two commonly referenced transactions do NOT work:

OMGN — does not exist in S/4HANA (ECC-era transaction)

OVXC — assigns shipping points for shipping point determination in sales orders. Per SAP Note 2698417, OVXC does NOT populate T001W.VSTEL. Many consultants waste hours here.

The correct path (confirmed in S/4HANA 2025 FPS01):

SPRO → Logistics General → Handling Unit Management →

Basics → Materials Management → Inventory Management →

HU Requirement for Storage Locations and Default Values for Deliveries

In this screen, select your supplying plant (PG11 in my sandbox) and assign the relevant shipping point (PGS1). This writes directly to T001W.VSTEL. Once saved, run /$SYNC, delete any stuck SMQ2 entry, create a fresh ODO — IS_HEADER_ORG will now populate with the shipping point.

IS_HEADER_ORG Root Cause Hierarchy (ranked by likelihood)

Priority

Root cause

Check

Fix

1 — MOST LIKELY

Shipping Point (VSTEL) blank for sending plant in T001W

SE16N > T001W > sending plant > field VSTEL

Assign shipping point via: SPRO > LE > Shipping > Shipping Points > Assign Shipping Points to Plants. Use TVSTZ table to find valid shipping points for the plant.

2

Sales Org not assigned to sending plant

SPRO > Enterprise Structure > Assignment > SD > Assign Sales Org - DC - Plant

Add entry for your sales org/DC → sending plant combination

3

Old SMQ2 queue entry — config changed after creation

Check FM name in SMQ2 queue container display — still /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA?

Delete old queue entry + delete old ODO + /$SYNC + create fresh ODO. Queue entries are frozen at creation time — they replay original routing.

4

OPIG doc type missing sales area context

IS_HEADER_DATA.DlvTy in SMQ2 — what delivery type is the system trying to create?

Verify /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE entry for your warehouse exists with correct Doc.Cat

 

Queue Container Display — IS_HEADER_ORG highlighted (yellow), right panel showing all fields empty: Delivery | ShPt | SOrg. | LoadPt | SOff. | WhN | SDst | Door | Unload.pt.

 

Section 4: EWM Warehouse Task Creation — Independent of ERP Replication

An important observation from sandbox testing: the EWM side of DODO (ODO creation, warehouse order, warehouse task, staging) works correctly even when the ERP replication is blocked in SMQ2.

This means you can validate and test all EWM-side DODO configuration (storage type, WPT determination, bin selection, warehouse task creation) before resolving the ERP replication blocker. The EWM and ERP sides can be debugged independently.

In my sandbox, Warehouse Order 3000003721 and Warehouse Task 1000006896 were successfully created against the ODO with:

  • Process Type: 2010 (Stock Removal)
  • Source Storage Type: DODO (staging area)
  • Source Bin: DD1
  • Destination Storage Type: Y920
  • Destination Bin: ST03 (staging bay for truck loading)
  • WO Creation Rule: DODO

 

Section 5: Configuration Summary Table

Config item

Variant 2

Variant 3

Doc.Cat=J required

S/4HANA 2025 status

Standard — confirmed

SAP recommended — confirmed

Blocked in standard ODSH

S/4HANA 2025 FPS01: Comm. to ERP field greyed out on copied ZDSH doc type — field lock root cause not investigated. /SCWM/ADGI recommended as practical alternative. Architectural value of ODSH documented for process control, HU lifecycle, and audit trail scenarios.

 

Conclusion

DODO is not a single process — it has four distinct variants, each with different document types, partner configurations, ERP communication paths, and function modules. Choosing the wrong variant before configuring is the single most common reason DODO implementations stall.

The key takeaways from this blog:

  • For plant-to-plant STO in embedded S/4HANA 2025: use Variant 2 (OPIG + plant BP). This is SAP's recommended path.
  • When IS_HEADER_ORG is empty in SMQ2: check T001W.VSTEL (Shipping Point) for the sending plant first — this is the most commonly missed field.
  • Always create a fresh ODO after SPRO config changes — old queue entries replay original routing regardless of what was changed.
  • EWM warehouse task creation is independent of ERP replication — validate EWM-side config before chasing the SMQ2 blocker.

For OPIG-based DODO (Variants 1 and 2): Picking status “Not Relevant” is by design — not a misconfiguration. GI must always be initiated from EWM (/SCWM/PRDO), never from VL02N in S/4HANA.

For internal goods movements without process control requirements: /SCWM/ADGI is simpler and sufficient. Use Variant 4 (ODSH) when you need a formal warehouse task confirmation step, HU lifecycle management, or a complete document audit trail — and first verify Comm. to ERP field availability in your system version.

 

I hope this blog saves you the hours of debugging it took me to arrive at this framework. Please leave a comment if you have encountered a DODO variant not covered here, if you have found a working path for Variant 3 (ODSH STO) in S/4HANA 2025, or if you have successfully configured Variant 4 with Comm. to ERP = B in S/4HANA 2025 — I would be keen to understand the field lock behaviour others have seen.

 

Happy Learning!

Yogesh Agarwal

Enterprise Logistics Execution Specialist | SAP EWM & TM | Warehouse Transformation | SAP MM/LE Consultant | MSCG | S/4HANA 2025 EWM

LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/ayogesh.

entry.title: Clarification Required: Transport Import Strategy for N and N+1 SAP Landscape
entry.content:
Hi Team,

We are looking for your help on SAP best practices for managing and sequencing transport imports across our parallel N (Business-As-Usual/Production Support/Minor Project) and N+1 (Major Project/Upgrade) system landscapes. We currently utilize SolMan ChaRM for transport management.

  • N Landscape: 4-tier (Dev-> QA->Pre Prod->Prod)
  • N+1 Landscape: 2-tier (Dev->QA)

We have two primary questions regarding our transport and retrofit strategy:

  1. Transport/Retrofit Approach: What is the recommended strategy for importing transports into the N+1 landscape? Can we perform the import/retrofit directly from the N Landscape’s QA system once we will get main TR after setting chaRM NC status as Successfully tested, or is it better to wait until the changes have successfully reached Production in the N landscape?
  2. Management Method: Should we manage N+1 landscape transports import from N landscape via the standard SolMan ChaRM retrofit process, or is it acceptable to set up a direct transport path from N (QA or Prod) to N+1 Dev, enable direct transport buffering, and schedule an automatic import job?

Please share any SAP official blog/Kbase, recommendations or best practices.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Clarification Required: Transport Import Strategy for...
entry.content:
Hi,

Regarding your concern, we recommend to do the retrofit once NC status as Successfully tested. After retrofit finished, you could do the import on N+1 system as normal process as N system, I mean post retrofit, you could create normal change for your N+1 landscape and import using task list as the N landscape does.

Check below documents for more details about retrofit functionality in ChaRM:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/sm/3518046800.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_Solution_Manager/8b923a2175be4939816f0981b73856c7/45d56775b9324fb79f103d55e44427b2.html

BR

This is a really smart use of AI. Turning CDS view data into a conversational analytics experience makes SAP S/4HANA a lot easier for business users to work with, especially when they do not want to depend on technical reports or exact query structures. entry.title: Re: Combining Agent Memory, Information Retrieval, and Human Knowledge Base - Karpathy's LLM Wiki
entry.content:
@ladenaw 

This is a really thoughtful topic. I like the way you separate agent memory, information retrieval, and a human knowledge base, because that reflects how good AI systems actually work in practice. The strongest agents usually do not rely on one source alone — they combine stored memory for context, retrieval for facts, and human knowledge for judgment and oversight. That balance makes the design feel much more realistic and enterprise-ready.

entry.title: Re: RISE Dashboard not showing data for SB1 system
entry.content:
As explains in the following guide, only the S/4HANA systems with product, test, development type will be supported in the dashboard.
The Sandbox/DEMO system will not be displayed in the list.

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/system-view

    The dashboard supports your SAP S/4HANA systems in production, test, and development. You can select only systems that are fully set up.

  • https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/faq-system-view

    Why are my demo systems not listed as a filter option under eligible systems?
    The dashboard does not support your demo systems. It supports your SAP S/4HANA systems in production, test, and development. You can select only systems that are fully set up.

entry.title: Program RFFOAVIS_FPAYM: No records selected
entry.content:
Hello Everyone,

We are currently experiencing an issue where the Payment Remittance is not being integrated into the SAP Business Network. Although we have already followed the configuration steps as outlined in the SAP Note (https://help.sap.com/docs/sisgw/sap-ariba-cloud-integration-gateway-configuration-guide/payment-remittance-request-configuration-in-sap-erp-and-sap-s-4hana), the payment remittance details are still not being transmitted to the SAP Business Network as expected.

When I did run payment the payment log has show message "Program RFFOAVIS_FPAYM: No records selected" , and we have look into this link Unable to generate remittance advice with report RFFOAVIS_FPAYM, and understand that The payment advice can be generated with RFFOAVIS_FPAYM if REGUH-XAVIS is set to 'X'. But still not sure whether incorrect configure or missing anything which made the root cause REGUH-XAVIS not set to 'X'. Hence, If anyone could provide guidance on how to resolve this issue, it would be greatly appreciated.

Best Regards,

entry.title: Re: Title: Residual Delivered Value Remains in PO History...
entry.content:
Hi Alinne,

Although all Goods Receipt postings have been reversed and the net GR quantity/value is zero, the Invoice Receipt is still active. Therefore, the delivered value may remain in the PO history due to the valuation and clearing logic, especially in scenarios involving multiple currencies (USD/CAD).

If the business requirement is to completely remove the delivered value from the PO history, reversing the GR documents alone is generally not sufficient. The Invoice Receipt and its related valuation impact must also be considered.

Best Regards,

Thu Mai

entry.title: Assistance Required for ASN Field CDS View
entry.content:
We have a customer requirement related to the ASN process. Currently, users enter the vendor invoice number in the External ID field, which is a free-text field. The business requirement is to prevent duplicate entries. If a user enters a vendor invoice number that has already been maintained for another delivery, the system should trigger an error message and prevent the duplicate entry from being saved.


To evaluate the feasibility of this requirement, I am trying to identify the relevant CDS view that stores the ASN number (External ID) for both SAP S/4HANA Inbound Deliveries and EWM Inbound Deliveries. However, I have not been able to locate the appropriate CDS view.


Could you please help us identify the CDS view(s) that contain the ASN number/External ID for S/4HANA Inbound Deliveries and EWM Inbound Deliveries?
